sentences.text: OK, yeah.
OK.
Under the darkness of night, and even this was forbidden, to go, under-- under death, under being-- under being killed.
He hides himself.
He goes to the house.
He hide himself.
In our house was 500 people-- 100 Jewish families, and three Christian families-- the administrator, the [NON-ENGLISH], the watchman-- and another woman.
And she had dogs.
And according to the-- to what I found out later, is the dogs started to bark.
They discovered my daddy.
And Yanowski, who was a Pole-- the administrator-- which was living on the Jews, called the Lithuanian. police and they-- to-- to get my father.
My father and my brother were together.
And the they went onto the attic.
And they hide themselves.
Finally, they found them.
Yeah.
Yeah, the older brother.
And finally, they found them.
And according to the story-- what I heard from-- from-- from the person what was watching the house after the war, that they put up quite a bit of a fight.
And they really beat him up.
And went over there, and they brought him to the police, and sent them to Lukiskes-- Lukiskes was a prison.
My mother found out about it.
And we heard there were some people which were working for the Lithuanians.
And they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they said, for the sum of 150,000-- 150 ruble gold, they will release my father from the-- from the prison.
My mother took the last penny from the house.
She went to the-- to the prison, to Lukiskes.
She never returned.
He took the money and put her into the prison.
And this is how my parents and my older brother was killed.
Now I am by myself, with the younger brother left.
They took them to Ponary.
Or they--
Yeah, so they killed them in Ponary.
Now I remained, myself and the younger brother, still alive.
Gershon.
And it started a struggle-- no money.
And the thing is, it's very-- I'm very-- we have to make a-- we have to find a way of be able to support ourselves.
So the thing is, we got first my brother joined the Brogansky, what was working for the Judenrat.
And he was working for him.
And my brother was, at that time, probably 13-- 12 or 13 years old.
And I was working in different job.
And the only way we could survive is to bring in from the outside, bring in some food-- food, or food stuff, to the ghetto, and sell at a higher price.
Now, the life is where-- it's rough.
Every single day, every couple days, the German are demanding people for Ponary.
There's a continuous so-called [NON-ENGLISH], or action, under the name [NON-ENGLISH].
They use a pseudonym.
They use-- they use different watershed so-called [NON-ENGLISH], to transfer to a different place of work.
Euphemisms.
But really, they took them to the Ponary.
And one of the stories happened-- one of the things happened during the yellow shines.
The Jews built, in order to survive, and to be-- to save themself from the deportation, they had to hide themselves.
This was in the beginning, when the idea was to hide, and to survive.
Our philosophy, for ages, was with our superior spiritual value, mind, intelligence, we can maybe survive over ages.
This was no match for the German.
The cruelty-- there was no-- there was no-- no-- there was only the murderous, a philosophy of destruction, of genocide.
And against it there was no-- no fight or wisdom.
And the thing is, in-- this was October the 23rd.
There was a so-called action for the yellow shines.
In this one, what the German had different tricks and they managed to give yellow shines to the people, with so-called professional.
And this one has a right to live, to life, and to save his family.
And the rest of it don't have a right.
This was a division between the people.
Why?
Because this one what has the yellow shine was for sure, I can live.
Why should I protect?
Everybody was for himself.
So it's a power of distraction, the power of what the Germans did, reached to a stage what everybody was for himself.
There was no communal-- communal, or organized sum of ideas.
So the thing is, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- they give about four-- in the ghetto was still about, I would say, 17,000, 18,000 people left.
Upon this one, they give only 3,000 shine.
This mean the total of 12,000.
Because each holder of this certificate could choose four people-- or three other people?
Yeah, so total four.
And, you know, everybody-- one was-- one family-- one even younger people used to sign-- sign himself with-- with to be married-- automatically they got married and have two children.
And this way they saved some of them.
And I would like to tell the story what happened.
I didn't have a shine.
So we hide themselves.
I was [NON-ENGLISH]-- for leaving [NON-ENGLISH] for hiding myself in the attic.
In the attic it was about 45 people.

And the thing is-- and Lithuanian, Ukrainian, Latvian, under the direction of the German community, surrounded the ghetto, came in to start the so-called [NON-ENGLISH], or action, or to [INAUDIBLE] about the ghetto people.
And we knew, already, from beforehand that this what will happen.
Because usually we used the trains.
And the German was-- used to organize, pretty well, that they are in an orderly fashion, to take the people to Ponary.
Ponary was only six or seven miles from Vilna.
But they took them by trains.
And we hide them-- we hide ourself in the attics.
The Lithuanians finally discovered our hiding, our [NON-ENGLISH].
This was a cry of a baby.
And they used to go from house to house, with the metal sticks, and knock on the door.
And one there, that you had over there the baby, and I will give you-- I wrote a story about it, too.
Over there, when they discovered our-- in the attic, the opening, the Lithuanian came in.
So the first thing what they did is they tried to get money, to be paid off.
So as they collected the money, one went out.
The second used to come in.
This one used to collect rings, and silver, and watches.
A third one, till finally they came, two or three together, and they used to throw-- and they forced out everybody-- with the stick, with the scream, with all the stuff.
In the beginning, they were very nice.
And this is the reason why they were volunteering for this type, especially everything.
But with it, I-- my-- my brother-- and I had still with my three cousins.
There was a opening in the attic.
So we jumped, and we crawl out, from the attic.
And we lay down on the roof.
The German and the Lithuanian saw us, from the road, because we had to cover ourself.
And they started to shoot in the air.
But they did not shoot against-- they didn't kill us.
My cousin, Rufka, got scared.
He slipped, fell down, and they took him to the-- [NON-ENGLISH], to the collection area.
And we four survived.
And everybody was taken to-- to-- to Ponary.
And this-- and in here, a tragedy was playing around.
In the same place was a father of-- of-- of my-- of my friend.
And he had a yellow shine.
And he assigned to his yellow shine a cousin, with her two children.
And though-- he sacrificed his-- his family.
He went to the second ghetto-- the German took them to second ghetto for three days.
And for three days, the [NON-ENGLISH], the call to action, was going on in ghetto.
Every single day, used to leave at dark and come at sundown-- at-- at sunrise.
And when they came in, they found-- come in to the place where we were living, he didn't find it.
He almost was in hysteric.
And the question arise how the German let you be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the decider who is going to live?
Because he-- he decided that the three people he would take would not be his-- was it his parents that he--
Yeah, he sacrificed his--
He sacrificed his parents for--
For his cousins.
Cousin and the two children.
And the parents were taken.
Yeah.
This is in here-- this is practically this doesn't happen in these story, where you can choose-- the Germans forced such-- such choices on the people.
There was a choiceless choice.
OK, in addition, there was another incident in the same-- why-- why do I mention it?
Because it's happen in the same thing.
There was a young woman, a young mother, with her child.
The child started to cry from the-- after the Lithuanian started to-- to hit.
In my malina.
And she took the baby to the breast.
And she tried to calm her down.
And when they calmed down, she pressed her probably against her breast, and was holding her.
And after they called everybody, and they took-- took this mother out from the malina, and she opened the-- the bag, the baby was dead.
This is a few-- only a few examples to give-- to give in here.
A friend, in contrary, had a similar.
And she put up a big resistance.
She was ready in the house.
She didn't go out there-- she went out to the Lithuanian.
She was ready to drop herself with a knife-- it was written of that.
Yeah, a baby.
She did not give up the baby.
She did not let anything in here it was-- and not only this.
When the babies-- they used to bring to the gates-- there was [NON-ENGLISH] was a [NON-ENGLISH] of ghetto.
He used to take the babies from the shoulder, from the head, and threw it on the wagon-- or to-- on the head, and hit them on the head, and threw them into the wagon.
OK.
This is now every single few days, we have-- we have almost [NON-ENGLISH], action.
They take-- they demand 300, 500 people-- old, children, et cetera.
I don't have a shine.
I'm almost to the point that it's hard even to breathe.
I had to do something, in order to survive.
This was an animal instinct to run away.
And at that time, we heard that at night some truck is coming and picking up some people.
And I went with my cousin and a friend, the same Schmulik-- the same what he described-- what I described about his family, what has the yellow shine.
And we went-- we heard that in Belarus it's still-- they are not killing people.
And they can move around, and they live still in their houses.
And I had a cousin.
I had an uncle, I'm sorry, in Nowogrodek.
He moved over there during the Russian.
And he was working for the-- on the railroad.
So we decided to go to Nowogrodek, because this is a place where they took-- took them, to Nowogrodek.
I'm not sure.
I'm not sure.
He was so fast.
He was so smart, even so young.
And he did not-- we were not-- you know, we were not going out, really, to go away.
We were going out just for trial.
And just in the moment, this came-- it's a-- it's a truck came in.
And he took us back into the people.
And boom, he went.
And he demanded 15 ruble gold from each one.
I did not have any.
But my friend took-- paid for me, and paid for Schlemke, what is my cousin.
OK, the truck was Anton Schmidt, we found out later.
This was a German.
And this was the only-- this was-- he was working for the city.
And he could not tolerate the injustice-- injustice of the-- of the-- what the Nazis were doing.
He sacrificed his own life.
He lost, after that, his life.
Yeah, he was doing it continuously.
This was not the first time.
He was doing it.
And this is the way.
But the problem is there was no way of running away from the Nazis.
They let us out in Nowogrodek.
From this truck.
There was about 40 people.
It was some families.
We three-- we already remained, without families.
We remained without anybody.
So we could pick it up and go.
But the rest of them were together.
Before you noticed-- it came out of nowhere-- came out the Belarus-- [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], this means the police, what were watching the street.
They took them in, into-- they brought them in, into synagogue.
They robbed them from everything.
And they kept them without food and eat for about two, three days.
And they killed them, in-- during-- in the-- in the-- in the place.
Not only this, we, at that time, being the first one detached from the other group, who heard the others from uncle.
We went in, in here.
He told us a story.
He told us in here, the population-- they don't kill yet.
We are still have quiet.
But it looked like anybody what comes in, they're already destroying.
Yeah, were still in their homes.
Probably about 30 miles, maybe even less, from Vilna to Nowogrodek.
Now, we grabbed the train.
And we got to Lida.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A little while later, we found out that they took the Jews from Nowogrodek, and they brought them on the edge of the-- of the-- of the forest.
And they all killed them.
And this I will tell the story afterwards.
But let me tell the story what happened in Lida.
In Lida, we came in.
To the first thing, you go to the Judenrat.
They give us a place where to live.
And they told us to go in to a family with three girls.
We were three boys.
In here, but they filled up-- it was like, hamlets, or like your houses.
The center of this town was completely destroyed.
And we are working.
I was working over there for [NON-ENGLISH].
And this is for [NON-ENGLISH] organization.
And now I will the two stories-- what happened in [NON-ENGLISH] and what happened afterwards.
We were building a depot for the trains.

And once upon a time, I was working-- I was working in the depot, and I brought-- and while I was going to work, I saw a group of Jewish girls, about 17, working as a-- we call this a brigade-- a brigade, to work.
And from nowhere, we saw the two young German soldiers were laying around, and were-- were target practicing.
And they were shoot with dum-dum bullets.
And they killed all 17.
And the bodies were flying.
And we were just pass them by.
And the blood and everything splashed all over in the ravines.
This a picture what still stays in my mind.
We went to work.
This is the same day.
This is '42.
This was the winter.
This was already-- the German didn't feel so good about this time.
Went in to [NON-ENGLISH] organization and while building in here, I brought in to my German engineer a long four-by-four.
He takes the four-by-four, hits me over my head, knocks me in my teeth, knocks me unconscious-- luckily-- and leave me over there.
Luckily, he left me.
Some of the guys came in later, and let me stand up.
And I lost my front teeth.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A little while later-- this was probably two, three months-- the Jews were still over there-- about 18,000, 17,000 Jews.
The Lida ghetto.
The Lida ghetto was not really a ghetto.
This was a big track-- same track.
And this one side were the Jews.
The other side was the Christians.
And the center was destroyed.
I see, suddenly, on the big track, near our house, a beautiful white stallion, with a German in black uniform, SD-- not S.
He probably was SD-- [NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH] was worse than SS.
And the-- and he was talking to the mayor of the-- of-- of Lida.
And I could not recognize.
He was-- he was-- he was gesturing, and moving around.
And I did not know exactly what happened over there.
But we knew already that this will be trouble.
Why?
Because they prepared already trains.
And this time, a lot of Lithuanian, Ukrainian, and Latvian, and maybe some Estonian came in, in town.
And we know that this will be destruction.
